teachingPoint: Capacity enforcement requires one atomic operation combining check and create. Separate check-then-write creates a race condition: two concurrent requests read capacity=1, both proceed to write, capacity becomes -1.
positiveExample: decrementAndCreate() = ONE atomic DB call. IR-63-1 / CF-802.
negativeExample: Read capacity → check if > 0 → create RSVP → decrement — three steps, race condition at boundary.
discriminatingConstraint: Atomicity is a correctness requirement, not a performance concern. No 'check first' step exists.
keywords: Capacity enforcement atomic operation combining check create Separate check-then-write race condition concurrent

teachingPoint: A full event (capacity=0) routes to waitlist — it is not a failure. DataProcessResult.failure means no path forward. Waitlist is a valid successful path.
discriminatingConstraint: A full event is a business state, not a system error.
keywords: full capacity routes waitlist failure DataProcessResult path forward valid successful

teachingPoint: When two trigger events produce the same business outcome via identical logic, they enter the same node. T63 handles both rsvp.requested (free) and payment.confirmed (paid) — same atomic operation for both. Separate nodes duplicate the logic.
positiveExample: Single T63 node, dual trigger. promotionRequest flag distinguishes path at T64→T63 call.
negativeExample: Separate FreeRSVPProcessor and PaidRSVPProcessor — duplicates atomic logic, divergence risk.
discriminatingConstraint: The trigger discriminates entry path. The business logic is shared.
keywords: trigger events produce business outcome identical logic they enter handles rsvp requested

teachingPoint: Waitlist promotion order is FIFO by joinTimestamp (MACHINE). Priority override is only available through FREEDOM config. Hardcoded priority logic is a CF-804 violation.
positiveExample: T64 promotion: first by joinTimestamp. Priority rules only via flow04_waitlist_priority_rules FREEDOM key.
negativeExample: Promote VIP members first — hardcoded priority. CF-804 violation. Fairness guarantee silently broken.
discriminatingConstraint: When fairness is a system invariant, it is MACHINE. Priority overrides are FREEDOM config.
keywords: Waitlist promotion FIFO joinTimestamp MACHINE Priority override available through FREEDOM config Hardcoded

teachingPoint: Lifecycle state transitions are triggered by state change events — not timers. T66 opens the feedback window on event.ended CloudEvent. CF-807: a timer fires at a pre-configured time regardless of event status.
positiveExample: T66 trigger: event.ended CloudEvent. IronRules BLOCKS timer/scheduler trigger.
negativeExample: Schedule feedback 2 hours after event start — if event runs long, feedback arrives mid-event.
discriminatingConstraint: Event-driven gates only. Timer is an approximation of state change, not the state change itself.
keywords: Lifecycle state transitions triggered change events timers opens feedback window ended CloudEvent

teachingPoint: Attendees who did not check in still complete the lifecycle with checkedIn:false. Their absence is recorded, not an error. FLOW-09 settles paid RSVPs regardless of check-in status.
positiveExample: T66 processes all CONFIRMED RSVP holders. No-shows → AttendanceFlowCompleted{ checkedIn:false }.
negativeExample: T66 only processes checked-in attendees — no-shows' paid RSVPs never settled. No error.
discriminatingConstraint: Absence is recorded, not an error. The lifecycle must close so transactional systems can process.
keywords: Attendees check still complete lifecycle checkedIn false absence recorded error FLOW-09 settles
atomic capacity enforcement decrementAndCreate race condition check-then-write oversell CF-802
teachingPoint: Capacity enforcement = one atomic call combining decrement + create. Separate check-then-write creates a race condition at the capacity boundary. Two concurrent reads of capacity=1 → two writes → capacity=-1 (oversell). The atomic operation is the ONLY correct design.
ironRules: decrementAndCreate() is ONE atomic DB operation — no separate read/check/write steps
Race condition window: any time elapsed between capacity check and RSVP write

keywords: dual entry node same outcome identical logic trigger discriminates rsvp.requested payment.confirmed
teachingPoint: When two trigger events produce the same business outcome via identical logic, they enter the same node. T63 handles both rsvp.requested (free) and payment.confirmed (paid) — same atomic operation for both. Separate nodes duplicate the logic and risk diverging implementations.
ironRules: Single node with dual @EventPattern when business logic is identical for both triggers
The trigger discriminates entry path — the atomic operation is shared
Separate nodes for identical logic = divergence risk with no local enforcement

keywords: lifecycle event driven gate CF-807 event.ended timer scheduler state change feedback window
teachingPoint: Lifecycle state transitions are triggered by state change events — not timers. A timer fires at a pre-configured time regardless of whether the prior state changed. An event fires when the state actually changes. CF-807 codifies this for FLOW-04.
ironRules: T66 triggers on event.ended CloudEvent — never a timer or ISchedulerService
Timer fires regardless of actual event state — approximation, not state change
CF-807: any setTimeout/setInterval/scheduler call in T66 is a BUILD_FAILURE

keywords: SETNX conditional update waitlist promotion WAITLISTED CONFIRMED status transition duplicate prevention
teachingPoint: SETNX (set if not exists) is correct for same-path duplicate prevention. It is WRONG for status transitions on existing records. When a promotion path needs to update an existing record (WAITLISTED → CONFIRMED), the operation must be a conditional update, not SETNX.
ironRules: SETNX for same-path duplicate prevention (attendeeId + eventId key)
Conditional update WHERE status=WAITLISTED for promotion path
SETNX on promotion path blocks legitimate promotion — record already exists

keywords: cancellation ordering RSVP CANCELLED capacity restore double claim window rsvp.cancelled emit
teachingPoint: Cancellation ordering: update RSVP → CANCELLED first, restore capacity second, emit rsvp.cancelled third. Restoring capacity before updating RSVP status creates a double-claim window where two attendees hold the same slot.
ironRules: Update RSVP to CANCELLED status BEFORE restoring capacity
Double-claim window: any code path where capacity restores before RSVP status updates
